domains.name: ELT with Spark SQL and Python

questions.stem: Which of the following BEST describes the key advantage of a Lakehouse architecture over a traditional data lake?

options.text: A Lakehouse stores data exclusively in proprietary binary formats for better compression
A Lakehouse adds ACID transaction support and schema enforcement on top of open file formats stored in object storage
A Lakehouse eliminates the need for any ETL processing by automatically transforming raw data
A Lakehouse requires a separate relational database to manage metadata for all stored files

questions.explanation: B is correct because a Lakehouse combines the low-cost scalable storage of a data lake with the data management features of a data warehouse — specifically ACID transactions, schema enforcement, and BI support — all on top of open formats like Parquet. A is incorrect because Lakehouses use open formats, not proprietary ones. C is incorrect because ETL/ELT processing is still required; the Lakehouse just provides a better foundation. D is incorrect because the Lakehouse metadata (e.g., Delta transaction log) lives alongside the data files, not in a separate relational database.
questions.reference: Databricks Lakehouse Platform overview

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to run a quick exploratory analysis on a dataset in Databricks. They need a cluster that persists between sessions and can be shared with teammates. Which cluster type should they use?

options.text: Job cluster
Single-node cluster
All-purpose cluster
High-concurrency cluster with autoscaling disabled

questions.explanation: C is correct because all-purpose clusters are designed for interactive, collaborative workloads. They persist between sessions and can be shared among multiple users running notebooks. A is incorrect because job clusters are created on-demand for a specific automated job run and terminated when the job completes — they are not meant for interactive use or sharing. B is incorrect because a single-node cluster has no worker nodes and is intended for small-scale local development, not team sharing. D is incorrect because high-concurrency is a historical cluster mode; all-purpose clusters with appropriate access modes replace this concept.
questions.reference: Databricks cluster types: all-purpose vs job clusters

questions.stem:
A junior data engineer runs the following command in a Databricks notebook:

```python
%run ./utils/helpers
```

What does this magic command do?

options.text: It imports the Python module `helpers` from the `utils` package using standard Python import mechanics
It executes the notebook at the relative path `./utils/helpers` and makes its variables and functions available in the current notebook's scope
It schedules the notebook `./utils/helpers` to run as a separate job task
It opens the notebook `./utils/helpers` in a new browser tab for editing

questions.explanation: B is correct because `%run` executes another notebook and imports all its variables, functions, and classes into the current notebook's scope. This is a common pattern for sharing utility code across notebooks. A is incorrect because `%run` is not a Python import — it runs an entire notebook, not a module. C is incorrect because `%run` is synchronous and inline, not a job scheduler. D is incorrect because `%run` has no UI effect; it executes the notebook in the background.
questions.reference: Databricks notebooks: %run magic command

questions.stem: A data analyst asks why their Delta table query returned data from two days ago even after new records were inserted. The engineer explains that Delta Lake uses a transaction log. What is stored in the Delta Lake transaction log (`_delta_log`)?

options.text: The actual row-level data in a compressed columnar format
A sequence of JSON files recording every operation (add, remove, metadata change) performed on the table
An index of all Parquet file column statistics used for query optimization only
The schema definition of the table stored as an Avro file

questions.explanation: B is correct. The Delta transaction log is a series of ordered JSON files that record every atomic commit to the table — including which Parquet files were added or removed, schema changes, and configuration updates. This log is what enables ACID transactions and time travel. A is incorrect because the actual data lives in Parquet files, not the log. C is incorrect because while the log does contain file statistics, it is not solely an index — it records all operations. D is incorrect because the schema is recorded within the log JSON entries, not as a separate Avro file.
questions.reference: Delta Lake transaction log (_delta_log)

questions.stem: An engineer needs to roll back a Delta table to the state it was in 48 hours ago due to a bad data load. Which SQL command accomplishes this?

options.text: `RESTORE TABLE sales TO TIMESTAMP AS OF '2 days ago'`
`RESTORE TABLE sales TO TIMESTAMP AS OF date_sub(current_timestamp(), 2)`
`RESTORE TABLE sales TO TIMESTAMP AS OF (current_timestamp() - INTERVAL 2 DAYS)`
`SELECT * FROM sales TIMESTAMP AS OF (current_timestamp() - INTERVAL 2 DAYS)`

questions.explanation: C is correct. The `RESTORE TABLE` command with `TIMESTAMP AS OF` reverts the table to a previous state, and Spark SQL interval expressions like `INTERVAL 2 DAYS` are the correct syntax for computing relative timestamps. A is incorrect because '2 days ago' is not a valid SQL timestamp expression. B is incorrect because `date_sub` works on dates not timestamps and `2` without a unit is ambiguous. D is incorrect because `SELECT * FROM ... TIMESTAMP AS OF` reads historical data as a query but does NOT restore (modify) the table — it just reads it.
questions.reference: Delta Lake time travel: RESTORE TABLE

questions.stem: A data engineer is configuring a new all-purpose cluster. Select TWO features that are configured at the cluster level rather than at the notebook or job level.

options.text: Databricks Runtime version
The SQL query executed in a cell
Autoscaling min and max worker count
The schedule cron expression for a notebook job

questions.explanation: A and C are correct. Databricks Runtime version and autoscaling configuration (min/max workers) are cluster-level settings set when creating or editing a cluster. B is incorrect because SQL queries are written inside notebook cells, not configured at the cluster level. D is incorrect because job schedules (cron expressions) are configured in the Jobs UI, not on the cluster itself.
questions.reference: Databricks cluster configuration

questions.stem: A team uses Databricks Repos to manage their notebook code. A junior engineer clones a repo and creates a new feature branch. After making changes, they notice the branch only exists locally in Databricks Repos. What must they do to share the branch with their team via GitHub?

options.text: Click 'Commit & Push' in the Databricks Repos UI, which commits changes locally and pushes the branch to the remote GitHub repository
Export the notebook as a .py file and email it to teammates for them to import into their own repos
Use `%sh git push` in a notebook cell, which is the only way to push to remote in Databricks
Merge the feature branch into main directly in Databricks Repos, which automatically syncs all branches to GitHub

questions.explanation: A is correct. The Databricks Repos UI provides a 'Commit & Push' workflow that stages changes, creates a local commit, and pushes the branch to the configured remote (e.g., GitHub). This is the standard Git integration flow. B is incorrect and defeats the purpose of Git integration. C is incorrect because while `%sh git push` is technically possible, the Repos UI is the supported and standard method — and the question asks what they must do, not what is technically possible. D is incorrect because merging into main does not automatically push other branches.
questions.reference: Databricks Repos: Git integration and push workflow

questions.stem:
A data engineer creates a table using the following statement:

```sql
CREATE TABLE monthly_summary
AS SELECT month, SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue
FROM sales
GROUP BY month;
```

What is true about `monthly_summary`?

options.text: It is a temporary view that disappears when the Spark session ends
It is a persisted Delta table that stores the query results at creation time
It is a view that re-executes the query every time it is queried
It is a global temporary view accessible across all active sessions

questions.explanation: B is correct. `CREATE TABLE AS SELECT` (CTAS) materializes the query results into a new persisted Delta table at the time the statement runs. The table stores physical data and does not re-execute the query on reads. A and D are incorrect because CTAS creates a table, not any kind of view. C is incorrect for the same reason — only views re-execute the underlying query on each read.
questions.reference: CTAS: CREATE TABLE AS SELECT

questions.stem: Which statement about `CREATE TEMP VIEW` is correct?

options.text: A temp view persists after the Spark session ends and can be accessed by other users
A temp view is scoped to the current Spark session and is dropped automatically when the session ends
A temp view physically stores its data in DBFS to improve query performance
A temp view requires USAGE privileges on the schema to create

questions.explanation: B is correct. A `TEMP VIEW` (or `TEMPORARY VIEW`) is session-scoped: it only exists for the duration of the active Spark session and is dropped automatically when the session terminates. No other session can see it. A is incorrect because temp views do not persist. C is incorrect because temp views are logical constructs that re-execute the underlying query; they store no data. D is incorrect because temp views exist outside of schemas and require no schema permissions.
questions.reference: Views in Databricks: TEMP VIEW scope and lifetime

questions.stem:
A data engineer writes the following query:

```sql
WITH active_customers AS (
  SELECT customer_id, name FROM customers WHERE status = 'active'
),
recent_orders AS (
  SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count
  FROM orders
  WHERE order_date >= '2024-01-01'
  GROUP BY customer_id
)
SELECT a.name, r.order_count
FROM active_customers a
JOIN recent_orders r ON a.customer_id = r.customer_id;
```

Which statement about this query is correct?

options.text: The CTEs `active_customers` and `recent_orders` are persisted as temporary tables that can be queried after this statement runs
CTEs are scoped to the single query in which they are defined and do not persist after the query completes
Each CTE is materialized to disk exactly once, so referencing a CTE multiple times always reads from disk
The `WITH` clause syntax is only supported when using Python DataFrames, not in Spark SQL

questions.explanation: B is correct. A Common Table Expression (CTE) defined with `WITH ... AS (...)` is scoped to the single SQL statement in which it appears. It is not persisted and cannot be referenced after the query ends. A is incorrect because CTEs are not persisted as tables. C is incorrect because Spark may or may not materialize CTEs — it can inline them into the query plan; there is no guarantee of disk materialization. D is incorrect because Spark SQL fully supports `WITH` clause CTEs.
questions.reference: CTEs in Spark SQL

questions.stem: A dataset has a column `email` that sometimes contains `NULL` values. A data engineer wants to replace `NULL` emails with the string `'unknown'`. Which expression correctly handles this?

options.text: `WHERE email != NULL`
`COALESCE(email, 'unknown')`
`email = NULL`
`IF(email, 'unknown', email)`

questions.explanation: B is correct. `COALESCE(email, 'unknown')` returns the first non-NULL value in its argument list — so if `email` is NULL, it returns `'unknown'`; otherwise it returns the original email. A is incorrect because `!= NULL` and `= NULL` (option C) always evaluate to NULL in SQL; the correct null check uses `IS NULL` or `IS NOT NULL`. D is incorrect because `IF(email, ...)` in Spark SQL evaluates the email string as a boolean condition, which is not the correct pattern for null replacement.
questions.reference: Handling NULLs in Spark SQL: COALESCE, NVL

questions.stem: A data engineer has an array column `tags` of type `ARRAY<STRING>` and wants to filter each row's array to keep only tags that start with `'product_'`. Which higher-order function should they use?

options.text: `EXPLODE(tags)`
`TRANSFORM(tags, t -> UPPER(t))`
`FILTER(tags, t -> t LIKE 'product_%')`
`FLATTEN(tags)`

questions.explanation: C is correct. `FILTER(array, lambda)` applies a boolean lambda to each element and returns a new array containing only elements for which the lambda returns true. `t LIKE 'product_%'` matches strings starting with `product_`. A is incorrect because `EXPLODE` unpacks array elements into separate rows, not filters within the array. B is incorrect because `TRANSFORM` applies a function to each element and returns a same-size transformed array, not a filtered one. D is incorrect because `FLATTEN` converts a nested array (array of arrays) into a flat array.
questions.reference: Higher-order functions: FILTER for arrays

questions.stem: A data engineer creates a Python UDF to clean phone number strings. Select TWO limitations of Python UDFs compared to built-in Spark SQL functions.

options.text: Python UDFs cannot accept string arguments
Python UDFs are not optimized by the Catalyst query optimizer, which can lead to slower performance
Python UDFs require data to be serialized from the JVM to the Python process and back, adding overhead
Python UDFs can only return integer data types

questions.explanation: B and C are correct. Python UDFs have two key performance limitations: (1) Catalyst cannot inspect or optimize the UDF logic since it is a black box, and (2) each row must be serialized from the JVM (where Spark runs) to the Python process and the result serialized back, which is a significant overhead compared to native Spark functions. A is incorrect because Python UDFs can accept any Spark-supported data type including strings. D is incorrect because Python UDFs can return any supported Spark data type.
questions.reference: Python UDFs: limitations vs built-in functions

questions.stem:
A data engineer reads a CSV file with the following code:

```python
df = (spark.read
  .format('csv')
  .option('header', 'true')
  .option('inferSchema', 'true')
  .load('/data/sales.csv'))
```

A colleague warns that `inferSchema` should not be used in production. What is the PRIMARY concern with using `inferSchema` in a production pipeline?

options.text: It reads the file twice — once to infer the schema and once to load data — which doubles I/O cost and may infer incorrect types from a sample
It only works with CSV files and cannot be used with JSON or Parquet
It automatically converts all columns to string type regardless of actual values
It permanently modifies the source CSV file to embed the inferred schema

questions.explanation: A is correct. `inferSchema` requires an extra pass over the data to sample and determine column types, doubling the read cost. More critically, it infers types from a sample of rows, which can result in incorrect type inference (e.g., inferring a column as integer when it occasionally contains decimal values) — making the schema fragile and unpredictable in production. B is incorrect because `inferSchema` works with multiple formats, though behavior differs. C is incorrect because `inferSchema` does the opposite — it tries to cast to specific types. D is incorrect because Spark never modifies source files during a read operation.
questions.reference: Reading CSV files: inferSchema considerations

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to speed up a join between a large `transactions` table (50 million rows) and a small `store_locations` table (200 rows). Which approach is MOST effective?

options.text: Repartition the `transactions` table to match the number of partitions in `store_locations`
Add a broadcast join hint: `SELECT /*+ BROADCAST(store_locations) */ * FROM transactions JOIN store_locations ...`
Convert `store_locations` to a view before joining to reduce its logical size
Use a `CROSS JOIN` instead of an `INNER JOIN` to avoid shuffle operations

questions.explanation: B is correct. A broadcast join hint tells Spark to send a full copy of the small table (`store_locations`, only 200 rows) to every executor, eliminating the expensive shuffle of the large `transactions` table. This is the standard technique for small-large table joins. A is incorrect because repartitioning the large table does not eliminate the shuffle — it just changes how it is distributed. C is incorrect because a view is a logical construct and does not change how Spark physically processes the join. D is incorrect because a CROSS JOIN produces a cartesian product and is never appropriate for a keyed join.
questions.reference: Broadcast join hints in Spark SQL

questions.stem: What is the difference between `CREATE VIEW` and `CREATE GLOBAL TEMP VIEW` in Databricks?

options.text: `CREATE VIEW` stores data physically; `CREATE GLOBAL TEMP VIEW` only stores metadata
`CREATE VIEW` persists in the metastore and is visible to all sessions; `CREATE GLOBAL TEMP VIEW` is accessible across sessions in the same cluster but is dropped when the cluster restarts
Both view types persist indefinitely and differ only in which schema they are stored in
`CREATE GLOBAL TEMP VIEW` requires admin privileges; `CREATE VIEW` does not

questions.explanation: B is correct. A `CREATE VIEW` (permanent view) is registered in the metastore and persists across sessions and cluster restarts — any session with access to the schema can query it. A `CREATE GLOBAL TEMP VIEW` lives in the special `global_temp` database, is accessible from any active session on the same cluster, but is dropped when the cluster terminates. A is incorrect because neither view type stores data physically. C is incorrect because they have different lifetimes. D is incorrect because global temp views do not require admin privileges.
questions.reference: Views in Databricks: CREATE VIEW vs GLOBAL TEMP VIEW

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to load new JSON files from a cloud storage path incrementally, without reprocessing files that have already been loaded. Which Databricks feature is BEST suited for this?

options.text: A standard `spark.read.json()` batch job run on a schedule
Auto Loader with the `cloudFiles` source format
`INSERT INTO` with a static file path
A `CREATE TABLE AS SELECT` from the JSON path run nightly

questions.explanation: B is correct. Auto Loader uses the `cloudFiles` source format to efficiently detect and process only new files arriving in cloud storage, tracking state in a checkpoint location to avoid reprocessing. A is incorrect because a standard batch read has no mechanism to track which files have already been processed. C is incorrect because `INSERT INTO` with a static path would attempt to reload all files every run. D is incorrect because CTAS creates a new table each time and does not track previously loaded files.
questions.reference: Auto Loader: cloudFiles source format for incremental file ingestion

questions.stem:
An engineer writes the following Auto Loader code:

```python
(spark.readStream
  .format('cloudFiles')
  .option('cloudFiles.format', 'json')
  .option('cloudFiles.schemaLocation', '/checkpoints/schema')
  .load('/data/incoming/')
  .writeStream
  .option('checkpointLocation', '/checkpoints/stream')
  .toTable('bronze_events'))
```

What is the purpose of `cloudFiles.schemaLocation`?

options.text: It specifies the path where Auto Loader writes the output Delta table
It stores the inferred schema so it persists across stream restarts and new columns can be detected as schema evolution
It defines the checkpoint location for tracking which Kafka offsets have been processed
It is required to enable wildcard paths in the `load()` call

questions.explanation: B is correct. `cloudFiles.schemaLocation` tells Auto Loader where to persist the inferred (or user-provided) schema so it remains consistent across restarts. Without it, the schema would need to be re-inferred on each start, which is slow and can introduce inconsistencies. It also enables Auto Loader to detect new columns in incoming files (schema evolution). A is incorrect — the output table is controlled by `toTable()` or `path` in `writeStream`. C is incorrect — `checkpointLocation` tracks stream progress, not `schemaLocation`. D is incorrect — wildcard paths are independent of `schemaLocation`.
questions.reference: Auto Loader: schemaLocation for schema inference and evolution

questions.stem: A data engineer is loading files into a Delta table using `COPY INTO`. They run the same `COPY INTO` command twice. What happens on the second run?

options.text: The second run fails with an error because `COPY INTO` does not allow running twice on the same target table
The second run reloads all the same files again, resulting in duplicate data in the target table
The second run detects that the files were already loaded and skips them, making the operation idempotent
The second run overwrites the target table with only the data from the second execution

questions.explanation: C is correct. `COPY INTO` is idempotent by design. It tracks which files have already been loaded into the target Delta table and skips them on subsequent runs. This makes it safe to run repeatedly without producing duplicates. A is incorrect because `COPY INTO` does not fail on re-execution. B is incorrect because idempotency is the defining feature of `COPY INTO` — it explicitly prevents duplicates from reloading files. D is incorrect because `COPY INTO` appends new data; it does not overwrite.
questions.reference: COPY INTO: idempotent file loading

questions.stem: A streaming job aggregates event counts per minute and writes results to a Delta table. The engineer uses `outputMode('append')`. Why might this cause incorrect results for aggregations?

options.text: Append mode writes all previously computed aggregation results again with each micro-batch, causing duplicates
Append mode only emits a row when it is finalized, but without a watermark, Spark cannot determine when a time window is closed, so aggregation results may never be emitted
Append mode is not supported for Delta table sinks and will throw an exception
Append mode converts the streaming job to a batch job, losing incremental processing

questions.explanation: B is correct. In append mode for stateful aggregations, Spark only writes a row to the output once it is considered final (no more late data can arrive). Without a watermark defined, Spark cannot determine when a window is closed and will therefore never emit aggregation results in append mode. Complete mode rewrites all aggregation results each micro-batch, which is why it works without a watermark. A is incorrect — that describes complete mode behavior relative to append. C is incorrect because append mode is supported for Delta sinks. D is incorrect because the streaming job remains a streaming job in any output mode.
questions.reference: Streaming aggregations: append vs complete output mode

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to process events from a Delta table as a stream. Select TWO valid options for controlling where the stream starts reading from the Delta table.

options.text: `option('startingVersion', '0')` to read from the beginning of the Delta log
`option('startingTimestamp', '2024-01-01')` to read changes after a specific date
`option('startingOffset', 'earliest')` to read all available Delta records
`option('startingPartition', '0')` to read from a specific Delta table partition

questions.explanation: A and B are correct. When reading a Delta table as a stream, `startingVersion` lets you specify which Delta log version to start from, and `startingTimestamp` lets you specify a point-in-time after which changes should be read. Both are valid options for controlling the starting position. C is incorrect because `startingOffset` is a Kafka-specific option, not a Delta streaming option. D is incorrect because `startingPartition` is not a valid Delta streaming option — Delta does not expose partition-level stream starting positions this way.
questions.reference: Delta incremental patterns: startingVersion and startingTimestamp

questions.stem: A streaming pipeline uses `withWatermark('event_time', '10 minutes')` for a windowed aggregation. A batch of events arrives that are 15 minutes late (their `event_time` is 15 minutes behind the current watermark). What happens to these late events?

options.text: The late events are included in the aggregation because Spark buffers all late data indefinitely
The late events are dropped because they fall outside the 10-minute watermark threshold
The late events trigger a recomputation of the entire aggregation from the beginning of the stream
The late events are written to a separate dead-letter Delta table automatically

questions.explanation: B is correct. The watermark defines how late data can arrive before Spark stops tracking state for a given time window. With a 10-minute watermark, events arriving more than 10 minutes late (relative to the current watermark) are dropped — they are not included in the aggregation. This trade-off allows Spark to bound the state size. A is incorrect because unbounded late data buffering would make the state grow forever. C is incorrect because watermarks are specifically designed to avoid full recomputation. D is incorrect because Spark does not automatically route late events to dead-letter tables — that would require custom implementation.
questions.reference: Watermarking in Structured Streaming

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to run a Python notebook on a schedule every day at 6 AM UTC. Where in Databricks should they configure this?

options.text: In the notebook itself, by adding a `%schedule` magic command at the top of the first cell
In the Databricks Jobs UI, by creating a job with a notebook task and setting a scheduled trigger with the cron expression `0 6 * * *`
In the cluster configuration, by setting the 'auto-start time' property to 06:00 UTC
In the Repos settings, by enabling the 'scheduled execution' toggle

questions.explanation: B is correct. Databricks Jobs is the correct place to schedule automated workloads. You create a job, add a notebook task, and configure a scheduled trigger using a cron expression. The cron `0 6 * * *` runs daily at 6 AM UTC. A is incorrect because there is no `%schedule` magic command in Databricks. C is incorrect because cluster auto-start is not a notebook scheduling mechanism. D is incorrect because Repos has no scheduled execution feature.
questions.reference: Databricks Jobs: scheduling and cron expressions

options.text: The entire pipeline run fails immediately with a data quality error
The record is included in `silver_transactions` but flagged with a warning in the pipeline metrics
The record is dropped from `silver_transactions` and counted as a dropped record in DLT metrics
The record is written to a quarantine table named `invalid_silver_transactions` automatically

questions.explanation: C is correct. `@dlt.expect_or_drop` drops rows that violate the expectation and records them in the DLT pipeline metrics as dropped records. The pipeline continues running. A is incorrect — that is the behavior of `@dlt.expect_or_fail`, which halts the pipeline on any violation. B is incorrect — `@dlt.expect` (without the `_or_drop` or `_or_fail` suffix) allows the record through and logs a warning. D is incorrect because DLT does not automatically create quarantine tables; that must be explicitly implemented.
questions.reference: DLT expectations: @dlt.expect_or_drop behavior

questions.stem: A multi-task Databricks Job has three tasks: `ingest`, `transform`, and `report`. The `transform` task must run after `ingest`, and `report` must run after `transform`. One execution of `ingest` fails. What is the default behavior?

options.text: `transform` and `report` are skipped, and the overall job run is marked as failed
`transform` runs independently since it is a separate task, and `report` runs after `transform` succeeds
The job retries `ingest` indefinitely until it succeeds
All three tasks run in parallel and the failure of one does not affect the others

questions.explanation: A is correct. In a Databricks multi-task job with linear task dependencies, if an upstream task fails, all downstream dependent tasks are skipped and the overall job run is marked as failed. This is the default behavior without any custom retry or error handling configuration. B is incorrect because task dependencies mean `transform` will not run if its upstream dependency `ingest` failed. C is incorrect because retries must be explicitly configured on each task; the default retry count is 0. D is incorrect because the tasks are configured as dependent, not parallel.
questions.reference: Databricks Jobs: task dependencies and failure behavior

questions.stem: A data engineer is designing a multi-task job where Task A produces data consumed by both Task B and Task C independently, and Task D runs only after both B and C complete. Select TWO accurate statements about this design.

options.text: This is a fan-out / fan-in pattern where Task A fans out to B and C, which fan in to D
Tasks B and C will run in parallel with each other after Task A completes
Task D will start as soon as either Task B or Task C completes, whichever comes first
All four tasks must run sequentially in Databricks Jobs — parallel execution requires separate jobs

questions.explanation: A and B are correct. This is a classic fan-out (A → B and A → C in parallel) and fan-in (B and C → D) pattern. Databricks Jobs supports this natively: Tasks B and C run in parallel after A completes, and Task D waits for both B and C to finish before starting. C is incorrect because Task D requires ALL upstream dependencies (both B and C) to complete successfully before it starts, not just one. D is incorrect because Databricks Jobs fully supports parallel task execution within a single job.
questions.reference: Databricks Jobs: fan-out and fan-in task orchestration

questions.stem: In Unity Catalog, how is a table `orders` in schema `sales` in catalog `prod` referenced using the three-level namespace?

options.text: `sales.orders.prod`
`prod.sales.orders`
`orders.sales.prod`
`prod/sales/orders`

questions.explanation: B is correct. Unity Catalog uses the three-level namespace in the order `catalog.schema.table`, so the correct reference is `prod.sales.orders`. A is incorrect because the order is reversed (schema.table.catalog). C is incorrect because it is in table.schema.catalog order. D is incorrect because Unity Catalog uses dot notation, not forward slashes.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog: three-level namespace

questions.stem:
A data engineer runs the following command:

```sql
DESCRIBE DETAIL prod.sales.orders;
```

Which information does this command return?

options.text: The full modification history of the table including who ran each operation and when
Table metadata such as format, location, size in bytes, number of files, and table properties
The column names and data types of the table
The SQL statement used to originally create the table

questions.explanation: B is correct. `DESCRIBE DETAIL` returns detailed metadata about a Delta table, including the table format, storage location, creation timestamp, last modified timestamp, number of files, size in bytes, and table properties. A is incorrect — that is the output of `DESCRIBE HISTORY`, which shows the transaction log history. C is incorrect — `DESCRIBE TABLE` (or just `DESCRIBE`) returns the schema (column names and types). D is incorrect — the DDL is retrieved with `SHOW CREATE TABLE`.
questions.reference: DESCRIBE DETAIL vs DESCRIBE HISTORY

questions.stem: A data analyst needs read access to the table `prod.sales.orders`. What is the MINIMUM set of Unity Catalog privileges required?

options.text: `GRANT SELECT ON TABLE prod.sales.orders` only
`GRANT SELECT ON TABLE prod.sales.orders` and `GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA prod.sales` and `GRANT USAGE ON CATALOG prod`
`GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON CATALOG prod`
`GRANT READ_METADATA ON TABLE prod.sales.orders` only

questions.explanation: B is correct. Unity Catalog uses a hierarchical privilege model. To query a table, a user needs `SELECT` on the table, `USAGE` on the containing schema, and `USAGE` on the containing catalog. Without `USAGE` on the catalog and schema, the user cannot traverse the namespace to reach the table even if they have `SELECT` on it. A is incorrect because `SELECT` alone is not sufficient — `USAGE` on the parent schema and catalog is also required. C is incorrect because `ALL PRIVILEGES` grants far more than the minimum needed. D is incorrect because `READ_METADATA` is not a standard Unity Catalog privilege for reading table data.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog: GRANT privileges hierarchy

questions.stem:
A data engineer creates a table with the following statement:

```sql
CREATE TABLE prod.sales.customer_pii
  (id BIGINT, name STRING, email STRING)
  LOCATION 's3://company-bucket/customer_pii/';
```

How does this table differ from a managed table in Unity Catalog?

options.text: There is no difference; the `LOCATION` keyword is optional and does not change table behavior
This is an external table: the data lives at the specified S3 path and is NOT deleted when the table is dropped from the metastore
This is a managed table: Unity Catalog takes ownership of the S3 path and will delete the data when the table is dropped
Using `LOCATION` creates a view over the S3 path rather than a physical table

questions.explanation: B is correct. When a `LOCATION` is specified in a `CREATE TABLE` statement, the table is an external table. The data files at the specified path are managed externally (in this case, an S3 bucket), and dropping the table from the Unity Catalog metastore removes only the metadata — it does NOT delete the underlying data files. A is incorrect because `LOCATION` fundamentally changes the table type from managed to external. C is incorrect because managed tables are created without `LOCATION`, and Unity Catalog controls the storage — dropping a managed table does delete the data. D is incorrect because specifying a `LOCATION` creates a physical table, not a view.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog: managed vs external tables
